<findings>

[observation] [info] High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
[observation] [info] Both fixture projects are realistic enough to exercise route detection, import graph construction, and component cataloging — maintaining at least one framework-based and one plain-TS fixture as the test suite grows will keep coverage representative.
[observation] [info] The Remix fixture includes nested route files (_auth.login.tsx, users.$id.tsx) which are valuable for validating file-system route detection; ensure the route-detection analyzer tests reference this fixture explicitly rather than synthetic data.
[observation] [info] Zero coupling confirms no production file imports from fixtures, which is correct; any accidental runtime import of fixture data would be a test-isolation defect to address immediately.
[suggestion] [info] Zone "test-fixtures" has files across 5 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory
[suggestion] [info] Add a fixture integrity test that asserts the expected internal import graph of small-ts-project — if fixture files are accidentally modified, import graph tests may silently pass with stale expected data rather than failing with a clear fixture-changed error.
[suggestion] [info] Document the Remix fixture's file-system routing convention dependency (Remix v2 _prefix and $ param syntax) in a fixture README or inline comment — undocumented fixture conventions break silently when route-detection heuristics are updated for a different router.
[pattern] [warning] Fixture projects spanning 5 directories with no centralized entry point risk being partially excluded by glob-based ignore rules — verify that sourcevision's own manifest ignore patterns do not accidentally suppress fixture files needed for e2e test coverage.

</findings>

<insights>

- High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
- The two fixture projects cover distinct technology profiles (framework-based Remix routes vs. plain TypeScript modules), providing good breadth for testing the analyzer across different import graph shapes.
- Perfect cohesion (1.0) and zero coupling (0.0) confirm fixtures are completely isolated from production code, as expected for inert test data.
- Fixture files should be excluded from sourcevision's own analysis runs to avoid circular self-analysis artifacts — verify that ignore patterns in the manifest cover tests/fixtures/.
- Both fixture projects are realistic enough to exercise route detection, import graph construction, and component cataloging — maintaining at least one framework-based and one plain-TS fixture as the test suite grows will keep coverage representative.
- Zero coupling confirms no production file imports from fixtures, which is correct; any accidental runtime import of fixture data would be a test-isolation defect to address immediately.
- The Remix fixture includes nested route files (_auth.login.tsx, users.$id.tsx) which are valuable for validating file-system route detection; ensure the route-detection analyzer tests reference this fixture explicitly rather than synthetic data.
- Zone "test-fixtures" has files across 5 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory
- The fixture zone's zero coupling confirms no production code accidentally imports test data, but the inverse risk also exists: if fixture projects share file paths with production ignore patterns, the analyzer could silently exclude them from fixture-based e2e validation runs.
- Fixture projects spanning 5 directories with no centralized entry point risk being partially excluded by glob-based ignore rules — verify that sourcevision's own manifest ignore patterns do not accidentally suppress fixture files needed for e2e test coverage.
- The Remix fixture's route files use file-system-convention naming (_auth.login.tsx, users.$id.tsx) that is only meaningful to the route-detection analyzer; if the route-detection tests do not explicitly reference these fixture files, the naming conventions are untested and may silently break if the route-detection heuristics change.
- The small-ts-project fixture contains inter-service imports (user-service.ts likely imports user.ts; email-service.ts likely imports user.ts) creating an internal import graph within the fixture. Sourcevision's own import analyzer tests exercise this fixture, meaning sourcevision is performing meta-analysis — analyzing an import graph specifically designed to be analyzed. If the fixture's internal import structure silently changes, tests may pass with incorrect import graph data.
- The Remix fixture files use file-system convention naming (_auth.login.tsx, users.$id.tsx) that encodes routing semantics in the filename; these conventions are specific to Remix v2 file-system routing. If sourcevision adds support for other file-system routers (Next.js App Router, TanStack Router), the single Remix fixture will not exercise the differences between routing conventions — add a second framework fixture or parameterize route-detection tests over multiple fixture projects.
- Add a fixture integrity test that asserts the expected internal import graph of small-ts-project — if fixture files are accidentally modified, import graph tests may silently pass with stale expected data rather than failing with a clear fixture-changed error.
- Document the Remix fixture's file-system routing convention dependency (Remix v2 _prefix and $ param syntax) in a fixture README or inline comment — undocumented fixture conventions break silently when route-detection heuristics are updated for a different router.
- [call graph] 5 internal calls, 0 outgoing, 0 incoming (cohesion: 1, coupling: 0)

</insights>
